title: Add Fractions with a Common Denominator
description: Add fractions that share the same denominator by adding the numerators and keeping the denominator the same.
condition: Two fractions with the same denominator are added (e.g., a/b + c/b).
response: Add the numerators and keep the denominator: (a+c)/b.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Add Fractions with Unlike Denominators
description: To add fractions with different denominators, rename them with a common denominator and then add.
condition: Two or more fractions with different denominators are being added.
response: Find a common denominator, rewrite each fraction as an equivalent fraction with that denominator, then add.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Add Measurement to Line Plot
description: Represent a new measurement by adding an X above the correct value on a line plot.
condition: A new measured value and an existing labeled line plot.
response: Locate the value on the horizontal axis and draw an additional X above it.
label: Production / Skill
example: If a new caterpillar measures $3\tfrac{1}{4}$ inches, add one X above $3\tfrac{1}{4}$ on the plot.

title: Add Mixed Numbers
description: To add mixed numbers, add whole numbers and fractional parts, renaming fractions as needed.
condition: Two mixed numbers are being added.
response: Add whole numbers and add fractional parts (using common denominators if needed), then combine.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Add Mixed Numbers with Unlike Denominators
description: Add mixed numbers by finding a common denominator for the fractional parts and combining whole numbers and fractions.
condition: Two mixed numbers or a mixed number and a fraction with unlike denominators are being added.
response: Rename fractions with a common denominator, add fractional parts, combine with whole numbers, and simplify if needed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes simplifying improper fractional results.

title: Add Quantities in Word Problem
description: Translate a word problem involving combined quantities into an addition expression.
condition: A context describes multiple amounts combined into a total.
response: Write and evaluate an addition expression representing the total.
label: Principle / Model

title: Add Unit Fractions with Same Denominator
description: Combine unit fractions with the same denominator by adding numerators and keeping the denominator.
condition: Sum of unit fractions with identical denominators (e.g., 1/b + 1/b + ...).
response: Add the numerators and keep the denominator fixed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Applies to any number of same-denominator unit fractions.

title: Apply Fractions Sequentially to Remainder
description: After removing a fraction of a quantity, compute a fraction of the remaining amount.
condition: A problem states that a fraction of a quantity is used, then a fraction of the remaining amount is used.
response: First subtract the initial fractional amount, then multiply the remainder by the next fraction.
label: Principle / Model
example: Start with $\frac{4}{5}$. Use $\frac{1}{2}$: remainder $\frac{4}{5} - \frac{2}{5} = \frac{2}{5}$. Then use $\frac{1}{3}$ of $\frac{2}{5}$.

title: Classify Value Using Benchmarks
description: Determine whether a sum or difference is less than 1, between 1 and 2, or greater than 2 using estimation.
condition: An expression involving fractions or mixed numbers must be compared to benchmark whole numbers (1 or 2).
response: Estimate the value and determine whether it is <1, between 1 and 2, or >2.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Uses rounding or compatible numbers.

title: Compare Division Expressions Using Structure
description: Compare two division expressions by reasoning about how the divisor affects the quotient without fully computing both.
condition: Two numerical division expressions are given for comparison (e.g., a ÷ b and a ÷ c, or a ÷ b and a × 1/b).
response: Determine whether one expression is greater than, less than, or equal to the other using properties of division and multiplication.
label: Principle
example: Compare $\frac{1}{6} \div 11$ and $\frac{1}{6} \div 2$. Since dividing by a larger number gives a smaller result, $\frac{1}{6} \div 11 < \frac{1}{6} \div 2$.
notes: Includes reasoning such as dividing by a unit fraction makes a number larger.

title: Compare Fraction to One
description: Determine whether a fraction is less than, equal to, or greater than 1 by comparing numerator and denominator.
condition: A fraction $\frac{a}{b}$ is given and must be compared to 1.
response: State whether $\frac{a}{b} < 1$, $=1$, or $>1$ based on numerator and denominator.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Core reasoning for multiplicative comparison. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compare Fraction to One-Half
description: Determine whether a fraction is greater than, equal to, or less than one-half.
condition: A fraction is compared to $\frac{1}{2}$.
response: State whether the fraction is greater than, equal to, or less than $\frac{1}{2}$.
label: Principle / Model

title: Compare Fractions Using Number Line
description: Determine which of two fractions is greater by comparing their positions on a number line.
condition: Two fractions are plotted or imagined on the same number line.
response: State that the fraction farther to the right is greater.
label: Principle / Model
example: On a number line, $\frac{3}{6}$ is to the right of $\frac{1}{4}$, so $\frac{3}{6} > \frac{1}{4}$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Compare Fractions with Common Denominators
description: When two fractions have the same denominator, compare their numerators to determine which fraction is greater.
condition: Two fractions with equal denominators.
response: The fraction with the greater numerator is greater; the one with the smaller numerator is less.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Used after renaming fractions to like denominators.

title: Compare Fractions with Common Numerator
description: When two fractions have the same numerator, the fraction with the smaller denominator is greater because the whole is partitioned into fewer, larger parts.
condition: Two fractions a/b and a/c with the same numerator and different denominators.
response: Determine that the fraction with the smaller denominator is greater.
label: Principle
example: Compare $\frac{7}{6}$ and $\frac{7}{3}$. Since $6 > 3$, sixths are smaller parts than thirds, so $\frac{7}{6} < \frac{7}{3}$.

title: Compare Fractions with Same-Sized Wholes
description: Determine which fraction is greater when the wholes are the same size by comparing numerators (for like denominators) or using a number line.
condition: Two fractions referring to the same-sized whole are given for comparison.
response: Identify which fraction is greater, less, or equal and justify using size or position on a number line.
label: Principle / Model
example: Since $\frac{2}{3} > \frac{1}{3}$, Gabe eats more than Robin.

title: Compare Numerical Expressions
description: Evaluate two numerical expressions and compare their values using >, <, or =.
condition: Two numerical expressions are given with a blank comparison symbol between them.
response: Evaluate both expressions and write >, <, or = based on their values.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}$ and $\frac{2}{3} > \frac{3}{4}$ is false, then write $<$.

title: Compare Products with Same Positive Factor
description: When two expressions share the same positive factor, compare the other factors to determine the inequality.
condition: Two products of the form a × c and b × c where c > 0.
response: Compare a and b and use the same inequality symbol for the products.
label: Principle
notes: Applies only when the common factor is positive.

title: Compare Sum to Benchmark Using Estimation
description: Estimate sums of mixed numbers or fractions by comparing parts to benchmark numbers (like 1 or 2) to determine if a total is greater or less than a given value.
condition: Given several fractional or mixed-number quantities and asked whether their total is greater or less than a benchmark without exact computation.
response: Use benchmark comparisons (e.g., each part is less than 1) to justify whether the total is greater or less than the benchmark.
label: Principle / Model
example: Since $\tfrac{7}{8}<1$, $\tfrac{5}{8}<1$, $1\tfrac{1}{4}<2$, and $\tfrac{1}{2}<1$, their total is less than $1+1+2+1=5$, and can be reasoned to be less than 4 using tighter bounds.

title: Compare Unit Fractions
description: Determine which of two unit fractions is greater by reasoning about the size of equal parts of the same whole.
condition: Two unit fractions (form 1/n) referring to the same whole (e.g., 1/2 and 1/3).
response: State which fraction is greater or less based on denominator size (smaller denominator → larger fraction).
label: Principle
example: Compare $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{3}$. Since halves are larger pieces than thirds, $\frac{1}{2} > \frac{1}{3}$.
notes: Requires understanding that more equal parts means smaller pieces.

title: Complete Equivalent Fraction
description: Find a missing numerator or denominator to create equivalent fractions.
condition: Given a fraction equality with one missing value, such as a/b = ?/d or a/b = c/? .
response: Multiply or divide numerator and denominator by the same number to determine the missing value.
label: Principle
example: Complete: $\frac{3}{2} = \frac{6}{?}$. Since $3 \times 2 = 6$, multiply denominator by 2: $2 \times 2 = 4$, so $\frac{3}{2} = \frac{6}{4}$.

title: Compute a Fraction of a Quantity
description: Find a fractional part of a given whole number or fraction by multiplying.
condition: A quantity and a fraction 'of' that quantity are given.
response: Multiply the quantity by the fraction.
label: Principle / Model

title: Convert Improper Fraction to Mixed Number
description: Rewrite an improper fraction as a mixed number by dividing the numerator by the denominator.
condition: An improper fraction a/b where a > b is given and a mixed number form is required.
response: Divide a by b to get quotient q and remainder r; write q r/b.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Remainder becomes the new numerator. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Convert Improper Fraction to Whole Number
description: Recognize when an improper fraction equals a whole number.
condition: An improper fraction with numerator a multiple of denominator (e.g., 9/3) is given.
response: Rewrite the fraction as a whole number.
label: Principle / Model

title: Convert Larger to Smaller Unit
description: Convert a quantity from a larger unit to a smaller unit using a known conversion factor by multiplying.
condition: A measurement in a larger unit (e.g., miles) must be expressed in a smaller unit (e.g., yards), and the conversion rate is known.
response: Multiply by the number of smaller units in one larger unit.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Requires knowledge of conversion rate (e.g., 1 mile = 1760 yards).

title: Convert Measurement Using Unit Rate
description: Convert between customary units using a multiplicative conversion factor.
condition: A quantity in one customary unit and a known equivalence (e.g., 16 ounces = 1 pound) are given.
response: Multiply or divide by the conversion factor to express in the target unit.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes division when converting smaller to larger units.

title: Convert Miles to Feet
description: Convert a distance in miles to feet using the fixed conversion factor.
condition: A distance is given in miles and needs to be expressed in feet.
response: Multiply the number of miles by 5280.
label: Concept
notes: 5280 feet = 1 mile is a memorized fact. [merged: condition varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Convert Mixed Number to Improper Fraction
description: Rewrite a mixed number as an improper fraction before performing operations.
condition: A mixed number must be combined with another fraction for addition or subtraction.
response: Multiply the whole number by the denominator, add the numerator, and place over the original denominator.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Often used as an intermediate computation step.

title: Count Data Points on Line Plot
description: Determine frequency of a measurement by counting the X marks above a value.
condition: A line plot with X marks stacked above labeled values.
response: Count the number of Xs above each value to determine how many data points occur there.
label: Production / Skill
example: If there are 3 Xs above $3\tfrac{1}{2}$, then three items measure $3\tfrac{1}{2}$ inches.

title: Count Equal Partitions on Number Line
description: Determine the denominator of a fraction by counting equal parts between 0 and 1.
condition: A number line from 0 to 1 partitioned into equal intervals.
response: Count the total number of equal parts to determine the denominator.
label: Concept
example: If the interval from 0 to 1 is divided into 8 equal parts, the denominator is 8.

title: Count Frequency on Line Plot
description: Determine how many data points occur at a given value by counting marks above that value.
condition: A line plot with stacked marks above labeled values.
response: Count the number of marks above a specific value to determine its frequency.
label: Category
example: If three X's are above $2\tfrac{3}{4}$, then its frequency is 3.
notes: Visual recognition and counting skill.

title: Count Shaded Parts
description: Count the number of shaded regions in an area model.
condition: An area model with some parts shaded.
response: Count shaded regions to determine numerator.
label: Production / Skill
example: If 10 of 18 parts are shaded, the shaded count is 10.
notes: Primarily perceptual counting.

title: Count Total Equal Parts in Area Model
description: Determine the total number of equal parts by multiplying the number of rows and columns.
condition: A rectangular area model partitioned into r rows and c columns.
response: Compute r × c to find total equal parts.
label: Principle
example: If a model has 6 rows and 3 columns, then total parts = $6 \times 3 = 18$.

title: Decompose Fraction into Unit Fractions
description: Express a fraction a/b as a sum of a copies of 1/b or other equivalent sums with denominator b.
condition: Proper fraction with numerator a and denominator b.
response: Write as repeated addition of 1/b or partition numerator into addends over b.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Includes multiple valid decompositions.

title: Decompose Mixed Number into Whole and Fractional Parts
description: Break a mixed number into a sum of whole numbers and a proper fraction.
condition: Mixed number of the form a b/c.
response: Write as a sum of whole numbers and the fractional part.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Often supported by number line representation.

title: Define a Unit Fraction
description: Recognize that a unit fraction represents one equal part of a whole partitioned into equal parts.
condition: A fraction of the form $\frac{1}{n}$ is given.
response: Interpret it as 1 out of $n$ equal parts of a whole.
label: Concept
notes: Foundational definition of unit fractions.

title: Denominator as Number of Equal Parts
description: Interpret the denominator as the total number of equal parts in one whole.
condition: A whole is partitioned into equal parts.
response: State that the denominator equals the total number of equal parts.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a square is divided into 4 equal parts, each part is $\frac{1}{4}$.

title: Determine Product Size Using Factor Relative to 1
description: Determine whether a product is greater than, equal to, or less than one factor based on whether the other factor is less than, equal to, or greater than 1.
condition: A product a × b where a is positive and b is compared to 1.
response: If b < 1 then a × b < a; if b = 1 then equal; if b > 1 then a × b > a.
label: Principle
notes: Assumes positive factors.

title: Divide a Fraction by a Whole Number
description: Compute the quotient when a fraction is divided by a whole number.
condition: An expression of the form $\frac{a}{b} \div n$, where n is a whole number.
response: Multiply the fraction by $\frac{1}{n}$ to get $\frac{a}{b \cdot n}$ (or equivalent simplified form).
label: Principle
notes: Often interpreted as partitioning a fraction into equal parts.

title: Divide a Whole Number by a Fraction
description: Compute how many fractional units fit into a whole number.
condition: An expression of the form $n \div \frac{a}{b}$, where n is a whole number and $\frac{a}{b}$ is a fraction.
response: Multiply n by the reciprocal of the fraction: $n \times \frac{b}{a}$.
label: Principle
notes: Conceptually counts how many fractional parts fit into the whole.

title: Divide by a Fraction Using Reciprocal
description: Divide a number by a fraction by multiplying by the reciprocal of the fraction.
condition: An expression of the form a ÷ (b/c) where b/c ≠ 0.
response: Rewrite as a × (c/b) and multiply.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies]

title: Divide Total by Number of Equal Groups
description: Find the value of each group by dividing the total amount by the number of equal groups.
condition: A total quantity and a number of equal groups are given.
response: Compute total ÷ number of groups to find the amount in each group.
label: Principle
example: If total weight is $9$ pounds for $12$ blocks, each weighs $9 \div 12 = \tfrac{3}{4}$ pound.
notes: Used to find equal block weight in Q5.

title: Divide Whole Numbers
description: Divide one whole number by another to find a quotient.
condition: Expression of the form a ÷ b where a and b are whole numbers.
response: Compute the quotient as a fraction or mixed number.
label: Principle / Model
notes: May result in improper fraction or mixed number.

title: Effect of Multiplying by Fraction Greater Than 1
description: Recognize that multiplying a positive number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the original number.
condition: A positive number is multiplied by a fraction known to be greater than 1.
response: Conclude that the product is greater than the original number.
label: Concept
notes: Response type treated as Constant because direction of comparison (greater than) is fixed.

title: Effect of Multiplying by Fraction Less Than 1
description: Recognize that multiplying a positive number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product less than the original number.
condition: A positive number is multiplied by a fraction known to be less than 1.
response: Conclude that the product is less than the original number.
label: Concept
notes: Response type treated as Constant because direction of comparison (less than) is fixed.

title: Evaluate Fraction Equality as True or False
description: Determine whether a statement claiming two fractions are equal is true or false.
condition: A statement of the form a/b = c/d.
response: Compare values (e.g., by using number lines or equivalent fractions) and state True or False.
label: Principle
example: $\frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{6}$ is False because $\frac{1}{6}$ is half of $\frac{1}{3}$.

title: Evaluate Parentheses First
description: Compute expressions inside parentheses before performing operations outside them.
condition: An expression contains parentheses with operations inside and outside.
response: Simplify the expression inside parentheses before proceeding.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Order of operations.

title: Express Fraction as Numerator × Unit Fraction
description: Rewrite a fraction as the product of its numerator and the unit fraction with the same denominator.
condition: Fraction a/b
response: Rewrite as a × (1/b)
label: Principle / Model

title: Find Common Denominator
description: Rename fractions with equivalent fractions that share a common denominator before adding or subtracting.
condition: Two fractions with different denominators are being added or subtracted.
response: Generate equivalent fractions with a common denominator.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Find Remaining Amount by Subtraction
description: When a total amount is given and some parts are used, subtract the used amounts from the total to find the remainder.
condition: A starting total is given and one or more amounts are used or removed.
response: Subtract the used amounts from the starting total.
label: Principle / Model

title: Form Fraction from Area Model
description: Express shaded parts over total equal parts as a fraction.
condition: Given s shaded parts out of t total equal parts.
response: Write the fraction s/t.
label: Principle
example: If 3 out of 20 parts are shaded, the fraction is $\frac{3}{20}$.

title: Fraction as Multiple of Unit Fraction
description: Interpret a fraction a/b as a copies of 1/b.
condition: A fraction with numerator greater than 1 is given.
response: Interpret it as that many copies of the unit fraction.
label: Principle / Model

title: Fraction Less Than One
description: Recognize that a fraction with numerator less than denominator is less than 1.
condition: A fraction a/b where a and b are positive whole numbers and a < b.
response: Conclude that a/b < 1.
label: Principle / Model

title: Generate Equivalent Fraction by Scaling
description: Create an equivalent fraction by multiplying numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number.
condition: Given a fraction a/b and a need for an equivalent fraction.
response: Compute (a×k)/(b×k) for some nonzero k.
label: Principle
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Identify Equivalent Fraction from Area Model
description: Determine the equivalent fraction represented when a shaded area is repartitioned into more equal parts.
condition: An area model shows a fraction a/b, then each part is subdivided into equal smaller parts.
response: Count total shaded smaller parts over total smaller parts to form the equivalent fraction.
label: Production / Skill
example: If $\frac{3}{4}$ is split into eighths, there are $6$ shaded parts out of $8$, so $\frac{3}{4} = \frac{6}{8}$.

title: Identify Equivalent Fraction Multiplication Form
description: Recognize multiplication expressions that are structurally equivalent to a whole number times a fraction.
condition: Expression of form n × (a/b)
response: Recognize equivalent form (n × a)/b
label: Principle / Model

title: Identify Equivalent Fractions Using Number Line
description: Recognize fractions as equivalent when they represent the same location on a number line.
condition: Two fractions located at the same point on aligned number lines.
response: State that the fractions are equivalent (or true) because they occupy the same position.
label: Principle
example: On aligned number lines, $\frac{2}{3}$ and $\frac{4}{6}$ land at the same point, so $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{4}{6}$.

title: Identify Fraction Equivalent to Whole Number
description: Determine when a fraction represents a whole number.
condition: Given a fraction a/b.
response: Recognize it equals a whole number if a is a multiple of b (i.e., a ÷ b is an integer).
label: Principle

title: Identify Fraction Represented by Unit Fraction Sum
description: Determine the fraction represented by a sum of identical unit fractions.
condition: Expression showing repeated addition of 1/b.
response: Count the number of terms as numerator over denominator b.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Inverse of decomposing into unit fractions.

title: Identify Fraction Represented on Number Line
description: Determine a fraction by identifying equal partitions between whole numbers and locating the point.
condition: A point is placed on a number line partitioned into equal parts between two whole numbers.
response: Count the number of equal parts to determine the denominator and count the position of the point to determine the numerator.
label: Principle
example: If the interval from 0 to 1 is divided into 4 equal parts and the point is at the first tick mark, the fraction is $\frac{1}{4}$.

title: Identify Greatest or Least Value on Line Plot
description: Determine the greatest or least data value represented on a line plot.
condition: A line plot is shown and the problem asks for the greatest or least value.
response: Locate the rightmost (greatest) or leftmost (least) data point that has a mark.
label: Category
example: If the rightmost X is at $2\tfrac{7}{8}$, that is the greatest value.
notes: Must distinguish between axis maximum and actual data point.

title: Identify Largest Data Values on Line Plot
description: Select the largest data values shown on a line plot for further calculation.
condition: A line plot is given and the problem asks for the heaviest or largest values (e.g., top 3).
response: Scan from right to left and select the specified number of largest data points.
label: Production / Skill
example: If the three rightmost X's are at $1\tfrac{1}{4}, 1\tfrac{1}{8}, 1$, select those values.
notes: Precedes summing in Q1.

title: Identify Shapes Partitioned into Equal Parts
description: Recognize whether a shape has been divided into parts of equal size.
condition: A shape is partitioned into multiple regions.
response: Determine whether all regions are equal in size.
label: Category
example: A circle divided into 6 congruent slices is partitioned into equal parts.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Interpret 'of' as Multiplication
description: Translate the word 'of' in a fraction context as multiplication.
condition: A phrase of the form 'a fraction of a quantity.'
response: Write and evaluate a multiplication expression.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Language-to-symbol translation.

title: Interpret Area Model as Fraction
description: Determine the fraction represented by a shaded area model by counting shaded parts over total equal parts.
condition: A shape is partitioned into equal parts with some parts shaded.
response: Write the fraction as (number of shaded parts)/(total equal parts).
label: Principle / Model
example: If 9 of 12 equal parts are shaded, the fraction is $\frac{9}{12}$.

title: Interpret Axis Interval on Line Plot
description: Determine the interval length between consecutive tick marks on a number line.
condition: A number line with evenly spaced tick marks and labeled fractional values.
response: Identify the difference between consecutive labeled values to state the interval length.
label: Concept
example: If labels increase from $4$ to $4\tfrac{1}{8}$, the interval length is $\tfrac{1}{8}$.
notes: Requires understanding of fractional increments.

title: Interpret Division as Measurement (How Many Groups)
description: Translate a context asking how many groups of a given size fit into a total into a division expression.
condition: A word problem asks how many groups of size $\frac{a}{b}$ (or other size) fit into a total amount n.
response: Represent the situation with a division expression (total ÷ group size).
label: Principle
notes: Also called measurement or quotative division.

title: Interpret Fraction as Division
description: Recognize that a fraction a/b represents the division expression a ÷ b.
condition: A fraction written as a/b representing equal sharing.
response: Write or interpret it as the division expression a ÷ b.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Core interpretation of fractions as division.

title: Interpret Fraction as Equal Parts of a Whole
description: Understand that a fraction a/b represents a equal parts out of b equal parts that make one whole.
condition: A fraction symbol of the form a/b is given.
response: Interpret it as a parts out of b equal parts of one whole.
label: Rule / Plan
example: $\frac{5}{6}$ means 5 equal parts out of 6 equal parts that make one whole.
notes: Definition-based understanding of fraction notation.

title: Interpret Fraction from Equal Parts Model
description: Determine or label fractional parts based on equal partitions of a whole in a tape diagram.
condition: Tape diagram partitioned into b equal parts with some parts shaded or bracketed.
response: Assign fraction a/b based on number of selected parts over total parts.
label: Production / Skill
example: If 4 of 10 equal parts are shaded, the fraction is $\frac{4}{10}$.
notes: Relies on visual part-whole interpretation.

title: Interpret Fraction of a Quantity
description: Interpret 'a fraction of a quantity' as multiplication of the fraction and the quantity.
condition: A phrase such as 'c/d of N' appears in a word problem.
response: Write and evaluate (c/d) × N.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Conventional language-to-operation mapping.

title: Interpret Numerator as Count of Selected Parts
description: The numerator tells how many equal parts are selected or shaded.
condition: A fraction a/b describes parts of a whole partitioned into b equal parts.
response: Select or count a parts.
label: Principle
example: For $\frac{3}{4}$, shade 3 of the 4 equal parts.

title: Interpret Shaded Fraction Model
description: Determine the fraction represented by shaded parts in a model.
condition: A visual model with some parts shaded out of equal partitions.
response: Express shaded amount as a fraction of the whole.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 1 out of 3 equal parts is shaded, the fraction is $\frac{1}{3}$.

title: Interpret Value from Line Plot
description: Determines the numerical value represented by a mark on a number line with fractional intervals.
condition: An X marked above a tick mark on a number line with equal fractional partitions.
response: Identify the fractional value corresponding to that tick mark.
label: Production / Skill
example: An X at the seventh eighth mark represents $\frac{7}{8}$.
notes: Requires understanding equal partitioning.

title: Interpret “Altogether” as Addition
description: In a word problem, recognize that 'altogether' indicates quantities should be added.
condition: A word problem asks for the total or uses language such as 'altogether' or 'in all.'
response: Add the given quantities.
label: Rule / Plan
example: David walks $\frac{5}{10}$ miles and $\frac{4}{10}$ miles; altogether means compute $\frac{5}{10} + \frac{4}{10}$.
notes: Language cue–based strategy.

title: Interpret “Left” as Subtraction
description: In a word problem, recognize that 'left' indicates subtraction from the starting amount.
condition: A word problem asks how much is left after some amount is used or removed.
response: Subtract the amount used from the starting amount.
label: Rule / Plan
example: A baker has $2$ pounds and uses $\frac{1}{3}$; compute $2 - \frac{1}{3}$.
notes: Language cue–based strategy.

title: Label Specified Fraction on Partitioned Number Line
description: Place or label a given fraction at the correct position on a number line divided into equal parts.
condition: Instruction to label a specific fraction (e.g., 1/3) on a number line from 0 to 1.
response: Partition the interval into the denominator’s number of equal parts and mark the numerator-th part from 0.
label: Principle
example: To label $\frac{1}{3}$, divide 0–1 into 3 equal parts and mark the first tick after 0.

title: Locate Fraction on Number Line
description: Place or identify the position of a fraction on a number line by partitioning intervals into equal parts.
condition: A number line interval and a fraction are given.
response: Partition each whole into equal parts and mark the correct point.
label: Production / Skill
example: To plot $\frac{6}{4}$, divide each unit into 4 parts and count 6 fourths to land at $1\frac{2}{4}$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Locate Improper Fraction on Number Line
description: Place a fraction greater than 1 on a number line by counting whole intervals and remaining fractional parts.
condition: An improper fraction a/b where a > b must be located on a number line partitioned into b equal parts per whole.
response: Count how many whole numbers fit into a/b and place the point accordingly.
label: Principle
example: $\frac{5}{3}$ equals $1\frac{2}{3}$, so it is located two thirds past 1 on the number line.

title: Match Decomposition to Target Fraction
description: Determine whether a given sum of fractions equals a specified target fraction.
condition: Given a decomposition and target fractions (e.g., 3/4, 4/3).
response: Evaluate the sum and select the matching target or neither.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Requires fraction addition and comparison.

title: Match Expression to Computed Value
description: Computes an arithmetic expression and selects the matching value from given choices.
condition: An arithmetic expression with provided answer choices.
response: Evaluate expression and select equivalent value.
label: Rule / Plan
notes: Involves computation plus comparison.

title: Match Visual Model to Unit Fraction
description: Match a partitioned and shaded model to the corresponding unit fraction.
condition: A shape or strip shows 1 shaded part out of $n$ equal parts.
response: Select or state the fraction $\frac{1}{n}$.
label: Principle / Model
example: If 1 of 8 equal parts is shaded, the fraction is $\frac{1}{8}$.
notes: Integrates counting equal parts and unit fraction meaning.

title: Measure to Nearest Quarter Inch
description: Determine an object's length and express it to the nearest quarter inch.
condition: An object aligned with a ruler marked in quarter-inch increments.
response: Read the whole number inches and nearest quarter-inch mark to record the measurement.
label: Production / Skill
example: If a pencil ends at the third small tick after 4 inches, its length is $4\tfrac{3}{4}$ inches.

title: Model Division as Equal Sharing
description: Represent division as distributing a total equally among a given number of groups.
condition: Word problem describing total amount shared equally among n groups.
response: Represent as a ÷ n and interpret as equal parts per group.
label: Principle / Model
example: 9 tablespoons shared among 4 loaves is $9 \div 4 = \frac{9}{4}$ per loaf.

title: Model Fraction Addition on a Number Line
description: Represent addition of mixed numbers as a movement to the right on a number line.
condition: A word problem asks for a visual or number line model of adding mixed numbers.
response: Start at the first value and draw a rightward arrow representing the second addend to show the total.
label: Production / Skill
example: Start at $1\frac{3}{4}$ and move right $2\frac{2}{3}$ to land at $4\frac{5}{12}$ on the number line.
notes: Graphical representation skill.

title: Multiply Two Fractions
description: Multiply two fractions by multiplying numerators and denominators.
condition: An expression of the form (a/b) × (c/d).
response: Compute (a × c)/(b × d) and simplify if needed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Multiply Whole Number by Fraction
description: Multiply a whole number by a fraction to get an improper fraction or mixed number.
condition: Expression of the form n × a/b.
response: Compute (n×a)/b and simplify if needed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Multiply Whole Number by Mixed Number
description: Multiply a whole number by a mixed number by converting the mixed number to an improper fraction or using distribution.
condition: Expression of the form n × (a b/c).
response: Convert a b/c to an improper fraction and multiply, or compute n×a + n×b/c.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Partition Whole into Equal Parts
description: Divide a whole shape or number line interval into a specified number of equal parts.
condition: Instruction to divide a whole into n equal parts.
response: Create n equal partitions of the whole.
label: Production / Skill
example: Partition the interval from 0 to 1 into 3 equal parts to show thirds.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Plot Value on Line Plot
description: Place a mark above the correct location on a line plot to represent a data value.
condition: A numerical value is given and a corresponding line plot scale is shown.
response: Locate the matching position on the number line and draw a mark above it.
label: Production / Skill
example: To plot $2\tfrac{5}{8}$, place an X above the tick labeled $2\tfrac{5}{8}$.
notes: Used when adding new data points.

title: Reason About Fraction Inequality
description: Determine which whole numbers make a fractional multiplication inequality true by reasoning about size relative to 1.
condition: An inequality of the form (n/5) × 16 < 16 where n is a whole number.
response: Conclude that n must be less than 5.
label: Principle / Model

title: Recognize Equivalent Fractions
description: Fractions that represent the same quantity occupy the same point on a number line.
condition: Two fractions can be simplified or scaled to the same value.
response: Conclude that the fractions are equivalent and represent the same quantity.
label: Principle
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Recognize Fraction as Sum of Unit Fractions
description: Express a fraction as a sum of unit fractions with the same denominator.
condition: Given a fraction with numerator greater than 1, such as a/b.
response: Rewrite as a sum of a copies of 1/b.
label: Principle / Model

title: Recognize Fraction Equal to a Whole Number
description: A fraction with numerator equal to the denominator equals 1 whole; a fraction where the numerator is a multiple of the denominator equals a whole number.
condition: A fraction where the numerator equals or is a multiple of the denominator (e.g., a/a or ka/a).
response: State the equivalent whole number.
label: Principle / Model
example: $\frac{4}{4} = 1$ and $\frac{16}{8} = 2$ because the numerator is a multiple of the denominator.

title: Represent Fraction of a Total
description: Express a shaded portion as a fraction of the total and multiply by the total to represent the quantity.
condition: A model shows a fraction $\frac{a}{b}$ of a total of $n$ objects shaded.
response: Write the expression $\frac{a}{b} \times n$.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $\frac{3}{5}$ of 25 circles are shaded, write $\frac{3}{5}\times 25$.
notes: Requires identifying correct fraction from visual model.

title: Select Appropriate Comparison Symbol
description: Choose >, <, or = to correctly compare two numbers.
condition: Two numbers with a known relative size.
response: Write > if the first is greater, < if less, or = if equal.
label: Concept

title: Select Appropriate Operation Symbol
description: Choose the correct arithmetic operation symbol to match a target result.
condition: Given numbers and a target result requiring an operation between them.
response: Insert the correct operation symbol (e.g., ÷).
label: Concept

title: Select Model Showing Valid Fraction Comparison
description: Choose a visual model that correctly represents two fractions of the same whole to justify a comparison statement.
condition: Multiple visual models are provided to justify a comparison between two fractions.
response: Select the model where both fractions refer to equal-sized wholes and are partitioned appropriately.
label: Concept
example: To show $\frac{1}{2} > \frac{1}{3}$, select two equal-length bars partitioned into 2 and 3 equal parts respectively.
notes: Response is constant per item (e.g., option C in Q4).

title: Shade Numerator Number of Parts
description: Shade the number of equal parts indicated by the numerator once the whole is partitioned.
condition: A whole partitioned into b equal parts and a fraction a/b is given.
response: Shade exactly a of the equal parts.
label: Production / Skill
example: For $\frac{5}{6}$, after dividing into 6 parts, shade 5 parts.
notes: Procedural representation skill.

title: Solve When a Fraction of a Whole Is Known
description: Find the whole when a fractional part of it is given.
condition: A situation states that a known quantity equals a fraction of an unknown whole (e.g., $a = \frac{1}{k} \times W$).
response: Divide the known quantity by the fraction (or multiply by its reciprocal) to find the whole.
label: Principle
example: If 8 quarts is $\frac{1}{3}$ of a cooler, then total $= 8 \div \frac{1}{3} = 24$.
notes: Requires interpreting fraction-of relationship before dividing.

title: Sort Shapes by Number of Equal Parts
description: Classify shapes based on how many equal parts they are partitioned into.
condition: Multiple shapes are shown with different partitions.
response: Group each shape according to whether it is partitioned into thirds, fourths, sixths, or not equal parts.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If a circle is divided into 6 equal sectors, place it under 'Partitioned into Sixths.'
notes: Requires counting and checking equality of parts.

title: Subtract Fraction from Whole Number
description: To subtract a proper fraction from a whole number, rewrite the whole number as a fraction with the same denominator and subtract.
condition: An expression of the form n − a/b where n is a whole number and a/b is a proper fraction.
response: Rewrite n as (nb)/b and compute (nb − a)/b; simplify if needed.
label: Principle / Model
notes: May require converting result to mixed number.

title: Subtract Fractions with a Common Denominator
description: Subtract fractions that share the same denominator by subtracting the numerators and keeping the denominator the same.
condition: Two fractions with the same denominator are subtracted (e.g., a/b − c/b).
response: Subtract the numerators and keep the denominator: (a−c)/b.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Subtract Fractions with Unlike Denominators
description: To subtract fractions with different denominators, rename them with a common denominator and then subtract the numerators.
condition: Two fractions with different denominators are being subtracted.
response: Find a common denominator, rewrite as equivalent fractions, then subtract the numerators.
label: Principle / Model
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Subtract Mixed Numbers with Like Denominators
description: Subtract mixed numbers that share the same fractional denominator by subtracting whole parts and fractional parts.
condition: Two mixed numbers with the same fractional denominator are being subtracted.
response: Subtract whole numbers and subtract fractional parts; regroup if necessary.
label: Principle / Model

title: Subtract to Find Difference in Context
description: Represent and solve a word problem asking how much more or less using subtraction.
condition: A context asks how many more or how much difference between quantities.
response: Write and evaluate a subtraction expression.
label: Principle / Model

title: Subtract to Find Missing Height
description: Find a missing stacked measurement by subtracting the known combined height from the total height.
condition: A total height and one or more component heights are given; one component height is unknown.
response: Add known component heights if needed and subtract from the total to find the missing height.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Multi-step subtraction of mixed numbers.

title: Subtract Whole and Fraction Parts Separately
description: Subtracts mixed numbers by subtracting whole numbers and fractional parts when no decomposition is needed.
condition: Mixed numbers where fractional part of minuend is greater than or equal to subtrahend.
response: Subtract whole numbers; subtract fractions with common denominator.
label: Principle / Model

title: Translate Context to Division Expression
description: Translate a real-world sharing situation into a numerical division expression.
condition: Word problem stating total quantity and number of equal groups.
response: Write division expression total ÷ number of groups.
label: Principle / Model

title: Translate Verbal Phrase to Expression
description: Convert a verbal mathematical description into a symbolic expression using correct operations.
condition: A verbal description involving terms like sum, difference, product, or of.
response: Write a matching numerical expression with appropriate operations and grouping.
label: Rule / Plan
example: "The sum of $\frac{3}{4}$ and the difference of $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{7}$" → $\frac{3}{4} + \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{7}\right)$.

title: Translate Word Problem to Addition Expression
description: Interpret a word problem describing combined quantities as an addition expression.
condition: A word problem describes two distances or quantities combined (e.g., walks 2/3 mile and then 1/6 mile).
response: Write an addition expression representing the total (e.g., $2/3 + 1/6$).
label: Principle / Model
example: Ryan walks $\frac{2}{3}$ mile and $\frac{1}{6}$ mile, so total is $\frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{6}$.

title: Use Benchmark Fractions to Estimate
description: Estimate sums or differences of fractions by comparing each fraction to benchmark values such as 0, 1/2, or 1.
condition: Asked to estimate a fraction sum or difference and classify relative to 1 or 2.
response: Approximate each fraction using benchmarks and determine whether the result is less than 1, between 1 and 2, or greater than 2.
label: Principle / Model

title: Use Parentheses to Control Order of Operations
description: Insert or interpret parentheses to ensure operations are performed in the intended order.
condition: An expression requires grouping to produce a specific value.
response: Place parentheses to group terms so calculations follow the intended order.
label: Principle / Model

title: Whole Number × Fraction as Repeated Addition
description: Interpret multiplication of a whole number and a fraction as repeated addition of the fraction.
condition: Whole number n multiplied by fraction a/b
response: Interpret as a/b added n times
label: Principle / Model

title: Write Whole Number as Fraction Over 1
description: Any whole number n can be written as n/1.
condition: A whole number is to be expressed as a fraction.
response: Write the number as n/1.
label: Principle
